findings.title: Database credentials stored as plaintext in ConfigMap
findings.description: Subtask 1008 discusses placing POSTGRES_URL (which may include the password) in the sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap. ConfigMaps are not encrypted and are visible to anyone with read access to the namespace. The description even notes 'POSTGRES_URL=postgresql://sigma1_user:$(password)@...' as a ConfigMap value. While the subtask notes the decision to separate password into secrets, the primary task description and multiple downstream services reference envFrom configMapRef sigma1-infra-endpoints for database connectivity, creating risk that credentials leak into ConfigMap.
findings.recommendation: Ensure POSTGRES_URL in the ConfigMap contains ONLY host/port/database (no credentials). All passwords must be injected exclusively via Kubernetes Secrets. Each service should construct the full connection URL at runtime by combining the ConfigMap template with the secret-sourced password. Audit all envFrom references to confirm no credential leakage into ConfigMaps.

findings.title: Inter-service authentication uses static pre-shared API keys without rotation
findings.description: The plan uses pre-shared API keys (sigma1-service-api-keys secret, subtask 1007) for inter-service authentication (D7 resolution). These are static symmetric keys with no built-in rotation mechanism. Subtask 2005 implements API key validation from environment variables. If any key is compromised, all service-to-service communication using that key is compromised. There is no key rotation automation, no key versioning, and no short-lived token exchange.
findings.recommendation: Implement key versioning by accepting both current and previous keys during a rotation window. Add a rotation procedure to the secret rotation runbook (10013). Consider migrating to mTLS with cert-manager for service-to-service auth in Phase 2 (already noted in 10010 as deferred). At minimum, generate cryptographically strong keys (256-bit), store rotation timestamps, and set up alerts when keys haven't been rotated in 90 days.

findings.title: Stripe webhook endpoint lacks signature verification in initial implementation
findings.description: Subtask 4007 implements the Stripe webhook endpoint (POST /api/v1/webhooks/stripe) and mentions signature verification, but one of the decision points explicitly asks whether to skip verification in v1. The Stripe webhook secret is provisioned (subtask 1007 has STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET), but there's a risk the implementation defers verification. Without signature verification, any attacker who discovers the webhook URL can forge payment confirmations, marking invoices as paid without actual payment.
findings.recommendation: Mandate Stripe webhook signature verification from v1 — this is not optional. The STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET is already provisioned. Use the stripe-signature header with the signing secret to verify every webhook payload before processing. Reject requests with invalid or missing signatures with 401. This is a direct financial fraud vector and must not be deferred.

findings.title: Signal-CLI REST API sidecar uses latest tag and exposes unauthenticated REST API on localhost
findings.description: Subtask 1010 specifies Signal-CLI sidecar image as bbernhard/signal-cli-rest-api:latest (unpinned tag from a community-maintained third-party image). The sidecar exposes an unauthenticated REST API on port 8080 within the pod. While it's localhost-only, any container in the same pod or any process that gains code execution in the Morgan agent container can send arbitrary Signal messages. Additionally, the 'latest' tag means the image could change without notice, introducing supply chain risk.
findings.recommendation: Pin the Signal-CLI image to a specific digest (e.g., bbernhard/signal-cli-rest-api@sha256:...) and audit the image contents. Configure Signal-CLI with authentication if supported, or implement a proxy sidecar that adds auth. Add NetworkPolicy ensuring port 8080 is only accessible from localhost within the pod (default for sidecar, but verify). Set up image scanning for the Signal-CLI image in CI.

findings.title: No user authentication system for web frontend and mobile app
findings.description: The web frontend (Task 8) and mobile app (Task 9) have no authentication system defined. The quote builder collects customer contact information but there's no user login, session management, or JWT issuance mechanism. The mobile app references JWT storage in expo-secure-store (subtask 9003) and token refresh logic, but no auth service or identity provider is specified anywhere in the plan. This means either all API endpoints are publicly accessible, or auth is an unplanned dependency.
findings.recommendation: Define an authentication strategy: either integrate an identity provider (Auth0, Clerk, Supabase Auth, or custom), or explicitly document that v1 is public-facing with no user accounts and all API access is unauthenticated. If unauthenticated, ensure rate limiting is robust on all public endpoints and no sensitive data is exposed. The mobile app's JWT references (9003) suggest auth was intended — this gap needs to be resolved before implementation.

findings.title: GDPR deletion endpoints lack authentication verification for customer identity
findings.description: All five services implement GDPR deletion endpoints (subtasks 2010, 3011, 4012, 5010, 6010) that accept a customer_id parameter and delete all associated data. While protected by API key auth (service-level), there is no verification that the requester is the actual customer or that the customer has verified their identity before deletion. The GDPR orchestrator (10014) also accepts a customer ID via CLI argument. A malicious internal user or compromised service key could delete any customer's data.
findings.recommendation: Implement a GDPR deletion request queue with verification: 1) Customer submits deletion request through authenticated channel (Morgan chat, email). 2) Request stored in audit.gdpr_deletion_requests with status 'pending_verification'. 3) Identity verification step (email confirmation, ID check). 4) Admin approval (Mike) before orchestrator executes. 5) Orchestrator only processes verified+approved requests. Add rate limiting on GDPR endpoints to prevent bulk deletion attacks.

findings.title: Multiple container images use 'latest' tags and community-maintained base images
findings.description: Several Dockerfiles and deployment specs reference unpinned or mutable tags: Signal-CLI uses 'bbernhard/signal-cli-rest-api:latest' (subtask 1010), Cloudflare tunnel uses 'cloudflare/cloudflared:latest' (subtask 1009), and Valkey uses 'valkey/valkey:7.2-alpine' (subtask 1005). The Rust services use 'rust:1.75-bookworm' (pinned to minor but not patch). None of the images are verified against signatures or checksums.
findings.recommendation: Pin all images to specific SHA256 digests instead of tags (e.g., valkey/valkey@sha256:abc...). Use only trusted base images (distroless, official runtime images). Add image scanning in CI (Trivy or Snyk) as specified in subtask 10012. Enable Kubernetes admission policy (Kyverno/OPA) to reject images without digest pinning. Create a documented list of all base images with their provenance and update schedule.

findings.title: Raw API responses stored in vetting_results JSONB without data minimization
findings.description: The vetting service (subtask 5001) stores all raw API responses in a raw_responses JSONB column for audit purposes. This may include sensitive business information, personal data of company directors (from OpenCorporates), and other PII that creates a data minimization concern under GDPR. The stored data could contain more personal information than needed for the vetting decision.
findings.recommendation: Implement data minimization: 1) Define exactly which fields from each API response are needed for audit purposes. 2) Strip unnecessary PII before storing raw_responses (e.g., redact director home addresses, personal email addresses). 3) Apply the same GDPR deletion to raw_responses when processing deletion requests. 4) Consider encrypting the raw_responses column at rest using pgcrypto. 5) Set a retention policy for raw response data (e.g., 12 months max).

findings.title: PgBouncer pooler exposed without connection-level encryption
findings.description: The PgBouncer Pooler CR (subtask 1004) is configured for connection pooling but there's no mention of TLS/SSL enforcement between application services and PgBouncer, or between PgBouncer and PostgreSQL. Database credentials transit over the cluster network in plaintext. While in-cluster traffic is somewhat protected by NetworkPolicies, this doesn't prevent a pod-level compromise from sniffing credentials.
findings.recommendation: Enable TLS on the PgBouncer Pooler CR using CloudNativePG's built-in TLS support (spec.pgbouncer.authQuery and TLS settings). Ensure all POSTGRES_URL connection strings include sslmode=require or sslmode=verify-full. Configure CloudNativePG to enforce TLS connections (pg_hba.conf hostssl entries). Verify Valkey also has TLS enabled or document it as a Phase 2 item.

findings.title: WebSocket chat endpoint authentication is vaguely defined
findings.description: Subtask 7009 mentions 'validate a session token or API key on connection handshake' for the Morgan WebSocket chat endpoint, but no concrete authentication mechanism is specified. The web frontend (subtask 8011) connects to this WebSocket. Without proper auth, anyone who discovers the WebSocket URL can impersonate customers, access conversation history, or send requests to Morgan that trigger backend service calls (catalog queries, quote generation, vetting triggers).
findings.recommendation: Define a concrete WebSocket authentication flow: 1) Generate a short-lived session token via an authenticated HTTP endpoint. 2) Pass the token as a query parameter or in the first WebSocket message. 3) Validate the token server-side before allowing message processing. 4) Implement rate limiting per WebSocket connection (messages per minute). 5) Sanitize all user input before passing to Morgan's LLM to prevent prompt injection attacks.

findings.title: No prompt injection protections for Morgan AI agent
findings.description: The Morgan AI agent (Task 7) processes user input from Signal, voice, and web chat channels and uses it to make decisions about tool calls (catalog search, quote generation, vetting, invoice creation, GDPR deletion). There are no mentioned safeguards against prompt injection attacks where a malicious user could manipulate Morgan into calling sensitive tools (e.g., sigma1_gdpr_delete, sigma1_create_invoice) or accessing data belonging to other customers.
findings.recommendation: Implement defense-in-depth against prompt injection: 1) Input sanitization layer before text reaches the LLM. 2) Tool-level authorization — sensitive tools (GDPR delete, invoice creation) should require additional verification even when called by Morgan. 3) Output filtering to prevent leaking internal system details. 4) Rate limit tool calls per conversation session. 5) Log all tool invocations with conversation context for audit. 6) Implement a 'confirmed action' pattern where destructive actions require explicit user confirmation.

findings.title: No security event alerting or anomaly detection
findings.description: While the monitoring setup (subtask 10020) includes infrastructure alerts (service down, error rate, replication lag), there are no security-specific alerts: no alerting on authentication failures, no detection of brute-force API key attempts, no anomaly detection for unusual data access patterns, and no alerting on GDPR deletion events.
findings.recommendation: Add security-focused Prometheus alert rules: 1) High rate of 401/403 responses (possible brute-force). 2) GDPR deletion event alert (always notify admin). 3) Unusual tool call patterns from Morgan (e.g., multiple GDPR deletions in short period). 4) Signal-CLI sending to unusual phone numbers at high volume. 5) Database connection from unexpected source. Add a dedicated security events Grafana dashboard.

findings.title: Invoice customer PII retention after GDPR anonymization
findings.description: The finance GDPR endpoint (subtask 4012) anonymizes customer_name and customer_email to 'DELETED' but explicitly preserves financial records for legal compliance. However, the invoice may contain PII in other fields: line item descriptions could reference customer-specific details, and the project_id links back to RMS where customer data exists. Partial anonymization may not satisfy GDPR's right to erasure if cross-referencing is possible.
findings.recommendation: Audit all invoice fields that could contain or reference PII. Anonymize project_id references or ensure the linked project is also anonymized by the GDPR orchestrator's ordered execution. Document the legal basis for financial record retention (typically tax law) and ensure the anonymization is sufficient that the individual cannot be re-identified from remaining data alone.

findings.title: Kubernetes secrets stored as base64-encoded (not encrypted at rest by default)
findings.description: Subtasks 1006 and 1007 create multiple Kubernetes Secrets. By default, Kubernetes stores secrets as base64-encoded (not encrypted) in etcd. Without encryption at rest configured at the cluster level, anyone with etcd access can read all secrets in plaintext.
findings.recommendation: Verify the Kubernetes cluster has EncryptionConfiguration enabled for secrets at rest (etcd encryption). If not, enable it. This is a cluster-level configuration, not a namespace-level one. Document the encryption status in the security runbook. If using a managed Kubernetes service, verify the provider's default encryption posture.

findings.title: No dependency version pinning strategy documented for Node.js and Go modules
findings.description: While Rust services use Cargo.lock for reproducible builds, the Node.js social engine (Task 6) uses 'bun install --frozen-lockfile' (good) but the Go RMS service (Task 3) doesn't mention go.sum verification or vendoring strategy. No dependency audit tooling is configured beyond the CI security scan.
findings.recommendation: Ensure go.sum is committed and verified in CI (go mod verify). Consider Go module vendoring (go mod vendor) for hermetic builds. Add 'bun audit' and 'cargo audit' as explicit CI steps alongside the security scan. Pin all npm/bun dependencies to exact versions in package.json (remove ^ and ~ prefixes) or rely strictly on lockfile.

findings.title: Multi-tenancy enforcement relies on application-level org_id filtering
findings.description: The RMS service (Task 3) uses org_id columns on all tables for multi-tenancy (D6 resolution). One decision point asks whether to enforce via PostgreSQL Row-Level Security (RLS) or application code. The plan defaults to application-level filtering which is error-prone — a single missed WHERE clause leaks data across tenants.
findings.recommendation: Implement PostgreSQL Row-Level Security (RLS) as a defense-in-depth layer alongside application filtering. Create RLS policies on all multi-tenant tables that filter by the current session's org_id (set via SET app.current_org_id). This ensures data isolation even if application code has a bug. Start with RLS in 'permissive' mode to detect violations without blocking, then switch to 'restrictive' after validation.

strengths: Per-service database roles with schema-level GRANT isolation (subtask 1003) provides good defense-in-depth for database access control
NetworkPolicy design is comprehensive with default-deny and explicit allow rules for each communication path (subtasks 10004-10008 and 1012)
GDPR deletion is systematically addressed across all services with a dedicated orchestrator and audit logging (subtask 10014-10015)
PodSecurity Standards restricted profile enforcement (subtask 10012) with runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem, and dropped capabilities
Multi-stage Docker builds with distroless/minimal runtime images reduce attack surface (subtasks 2013, 3013, 4014, 5012)
Structured audit logging with differentiated retention (90 days standard, 1 year audit) via Loki (subtask 10021)
API key authentication middleware is implemented as a shared component reused across all Rust services (subtask 2005)
Circuit breaker pattern in vetting service (subtask 5003) prevents cascade failures and provides graceful degradation
The plan separates database credentials into per-service roles rather than sharing a single superuser credential
summary: The plan demonstrates solid infrastructure security fundamentals with NetworkPolicies, PodSecurity Standards, per-service database roles, and GDPR compliance architecture. However, there are critical gaps: Stripe webhook signature verification must not be deferred (direct financial fraud risk), the complete absence of end-user authentication for web/mobile clients is a significant architectural gap, and the Morgan AI agent lacks prompt injection protections despite having access to sensitive tools including GDPR deletion and invoice creation. The inter-service auth model using static pre-shared keys should be strengthened with rotation automation and eventually replaced with mTLS.
